title: Unraveling the Origin of Ferrimagnetic Signatures in (Fe,Mn,Ga)2O3 Bixbyites: The Role of Structurally-Undetectable Spinel Impurities
authors: Evgeniya Moshkina, Yuriy Knyazev, Ekaterina Smorodina, Oleg Bayukov, Maxim Molokeev, Evgeniy Khramov, Andrey Kartashev, Ruslan Batulin, Mikhail Cherosov, Dmitriy Velikanov, Evgeniy Eremin, Mikhail Rautskii, Dieter Kokh, Mikhail Platunov, Leonard Bezmaternykh
abstract: The cubic Fe2-xMnxO3 is an intriguing material that has recently been investigated for various applications, including lithium-ion battery anodes, catalysts, energy storage media, humidity sensors, and photocatalysts. Despite its wide range of promising applications, the magnetic properties of Fe2-xMnxO3 remain controversial, with different sources reporting conflicting information regarding the type of magnetic ordering, phase transition temperature, and magnetic moment of this compound. This work presents a study of the magnetic state of three Fe2-xMnxO3:Ga solid solutions with varying Mn:Fe:Ga ratios, along with one gallium-free Fe2-xMnxO3 reference sample. We performed a detailed analysis of the actual chemical composition and crystal structure of the synthesized samples using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to evaluate compositional differences. The magnetic states of the three Fe2-xMnxO3:Ga samples and the gallium-free Fe2-xMnxO3 were investigated using magnetometry and Mossbauer spectroscopy. The low-temperature magnetic anomalies were found to be more consistent with spin-glass-like freezing than with conventional long-range antiferromagnetic ordering. Although variations in magnetic behavior were observed and found to depend on composition and the cooling rate during synthesis, our results demonstrate that these factors do not account for the drastically different magnetic properties reported for similar bixbyite-type oxides. Instead, the apparent room-temperature ferrimagnetism observed in one sample is most likely extrinsic and can be attributed to a trace spinel-type impurity phase, as supported by magnetizations and ESR measurements. Thus, the origin of these discrepancies lies primarily in the chemical purity of the samples and, to a significant extent, in the synthesis technique employed.
summary: This paper investigates the magnetic properties of (Fe,Mn,Ga)2O3 bixbyites to resolve discrepancies in literature regarding their magnetic ordering. It demonstrates that the reported room-temperature ferrimagnetism is actually due to trace spinel impurities rather than an intrinsic property of the bixbyite phase. The study also identifies low-temperature magnetic anomalies as spin-glass-like freezing.
main_problem: To resolve conflicting reports regarding the magnetic ordering of (Fe,Mn,Ga)2O3 bixbyites and determine if the observed room-temperature ferrimagnetism is an intrinsic property or caused by impurities.
main_result: The study concludes that the low-temperature magnetic anomalies are consistent with spin-glass-like freezing rather than long-range antiferromagnetism, and the reported room-temperature ferrimagnetism is an extrinsic effect caused by trace spinel-type impurities.
method: The researchers used flux-grown single crystals and employed a combination of magnetometry, Mossbauer spectroscopy, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), Electron Spin Resonance (ESR), and specific heat measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of cubic (Fe,Mn,Ga)2O3 bixbyites (space group Ia) with varying Mn:Fe:Ga ratios, characterized by two inequivalent octahedral metal sites (M1 and M2) and significant cation disorder.
key_observables: Magnetic susceptibility, magnetization, magnetic transition temperature (TN and TC), effective magnetic moment, hyperfine parameters (isomer shift, quadrupole splitting), and ESR g-factors.
important_parameters: Cation distribution (8b and 24d sites), Mn:Fe:Ga ratios, cooling rates during synthesis, and the presence of spinel-type impurity phases.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed high-temperature magnetic signal in certain samples can be modeled by subtracting a scaled contribution from a spinel impurity phase.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the bixbyite crystal structure; Figure 2 displays temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility; Figure 3 shows field-dependent magnetization; Figure 7 presents XANES spectra; Figure 8 shows EXAFS Fourier transforms; Figure 9 illustrates temperature derivatives of magnetic properties.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the controversy in bixbyite magnetism, describes the synthesis and structural characterization of various Fe-Mn-Ga oxides, presents magnetic and spectroscopic results to analyze low-temperature spin-glass behavior, identifies the spinel impurity as the source of ferrimagnetism, and concludes with the impact of chemical purity on reported properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Fe2-xMnxO3 structure. The bixbyites have 3 Ia space group, the unit cell contains two formula units and two nonequivalent octahedral positions occupied by transitional metals. Purple and green spheres – M1 and M2 metal positions, respectively (M1, M2 = Mn, Fe); red spheres – oxygen.
